title: Snickerdoodles
subtitle: Soft American biscuits rolled in cinnamon sugar
excerpt: American snickerdoodles: soft cinnamon-sugar biscuits with cream of tartar in the dough for the characteristic tang. Twenty biscuits, rolled in cinnamon sugar before baking and ready in under thirty minutes.
sourceNotes: Fannie Farmer, The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896), Project Gutenberg: documents the cream-of-tartar-and-bicarbonate biscuit base. The Joy of Cooking first edition (1931, public domain in segments): notes the cinnamon-sugar roll as the canonical finish for the snickerdoodle. The name appears in late nineteenth-century New England household cookbooks; the recipe form is a soft cream-of-tartar drop biscuit.

recipe.freezeNotes: Bake, cool, freeze in a tin for up to 3 months. Soft biscuits freeze well.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough holds in the fridge for a day, wrapped; bring to cool room temperature before rolling.

recipeTools.notes: A hand mixer or wooden spoon works just as well.

glossaryTerms.definition: Cream of tartar (potassium hydrogen tartrate) is a mild acid sold as a fine white powder, by-product of wine-making. Paired with bicarbonate of soda it makes a homemade baking powder and gives a slightly tangy flavour to soft biscuits. The tang is what distinguishes a snickerdoodle from a plain sugar biscuit; without it the result is just a cinnamon-sugar cookie.

content.text: Snickerdoodles are an American household biscuit from the late nineteenth century: a soft creamed-butter dough leavened with cream of tartar and bicarbonate of soda, rolled into balls, tossed in cinnamon sugar, and baked just until the edges set. The tang of the cream of tartar against the cinnamon is what makes them snickerdoodles rather than plain cinnamon cookies.
Best on the day they are baked, when the centres are still soft. The cinnamon-sugar coating gives a crisp shell against the soft middle.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan and line two baking trays. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and the first 200 g of {{caster-sugar}} until pale and creamed; this takes 2–3 minutes by hand. Beat in the {{eggs}} and {{vanilla-extract}}. Sift in the {{plain-flour}}, {{cream-of-tartar}} (the

), {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} and mix to a soft dough. Mix the remaining {{caster-sugar}} with the {{cinnamon-ground}} in a shallow bowl. Roll the dough into 20 walnut-sized balls and toss each in the cinnamon sugar to coat fully.

Set the balls on the trays with 4 cm between each. Bake for 8–10 minutes; the biscuits should puff up and crack on top, then settle. The edges should be set, the centres still soft. Cool 5 minutes on the tray, then transfer to a rack.
